data.title: Veolia Environnement: Release of the Base Prospectus of the 2025 “Euro Medium Term Notes” Programme
data.description:
Veolia Environnement: Release of the Base Prospectus of the 2025 “Euro Medium Term Notes” Programme

Regulatory News:

Veolia Environnement (Paris:VIE) has finalized the annual update of its 20 billion euros Euro Medium Term Notes program.

The base prospectus (AMF approval number 25-084 on March, 28th 2025) is available on the website of the company www.veolia.com (“finance” area, section “debt and ratings” under “analysts and investors”), at its administrative headquarters, from the paying agent (Société Générale Securities Services) and on the website of the Autorité des Marchés Financiers www.amf-france.org.

ABOUT VEOLIA

Veolia group aims to become the benchmark company for ecological transformation. Present on five continents with 215,000 employees, the Group designs and deploys useful, practical solutions for the management of water, waste and energy that are contributing to a radical turnaround of the current situation. Through its three complementary activities, Veolia helps to develop access to resources, to preserve available resources and to renew them. In 2024, the Veolia group provided 111 million inhabitants with drinking water and 98 million with sanitation, produced 42 million megawatt hours of energy and treated 65 million tonnes of waste. Veolia Environnement (Paris Euronext: VIE) achieved consolidated revenue of 44.7 billion euros in 2024. www.veolia.com

CONTACTS VEOLIA

RELATIONS PRESSE GROUPE
Laurent Obadia – Evgeniya Mazalova
Charline Bouchereau – Anna Beaubatie
Aurélien Sarrosquy
+33 (0)1 85 57 86 25

data.title: Eoptolink Launches 1.6T OSFP 2VR4 Transceivers Using 200G VCSELs
data.description:
Eoptolink Launches 1.6T OSFP 2VR4 Transceivers Using 200G VCSELs

Eoptolink Launches 1.6T OSFP 2VR4 Transceivers Using 200G VCSELs

PR Newswire

SAN FRANCISCO, March 29, 2025

SAN FRANCISCO, March 29, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Eoptolink Technology Inc., Ltd. (SZSE: 300502), a leading innovator and provider of advanced optical transceiver solutions, is demonstrating its 1.6T multimode transceiver operating at 212Gb/s per lambda during OFC 2025.

Vertical Cavity Surface Emitting Lasers (VCSELs) operating at 850nm are the dominant optical laser technology for less than 100m transmission distance of short reach connectivity. At OFC 2025 in San Francisco, Eoptolink is launching its 1.6T OSFP 2VR4 optical transceiver.

VCSEL lasers operating at 106Gb/s are incorporated in many of today's short reach links built with 400G SR4 and 800G SR8 optical transceivers. The next evolutionary step is to increase the data-rate in these connections to 212 Gb/s.

The 1.6T OSP 2VR4 transceiver has two optical MPO-12 interfaces each operating at 800G using optical four lanes at 212Gb/s. The transceiver offers a suitable method to migrate legacy multimode installations to newer switch platforms.

"Eoptolink continues to lead the way as an optical transceiver innovator and we are excited to get early access to this 200G VCSEL technology", explains Dirk Lutz, Distinguished Engineer, Eoptolink Technology Inc., Ltd. "Eoptolink has an excellent execution track record, which is one of the key reasons why companies choose to partner with Eoptolink to explore the cutting-edge technologies."

We will be showing live demonstrations of a 1.6T, 800G, LPO, LRO and MCF optical transceiver solutions, at the Eoptolink booth #2943 at OFC 2025, San Francisco, CA and welcome to visit us.

About Eoptolink

Eoptolink Technology Inc., Ltd. (SZSE: 300502) is a leading innovator and provider of advanced optical transceiver solutions for data center, enterprise and telecom networks. Eoptolink is dedicated to research, develop, manufacture and markets a diverse portfolio of high-performance optical transceivers for AI/ML Cluster, Cloud Data Center, 4G/5G wireless, Transport & Datacom and FTTX applications all over the world.

Contact Us

USA:           3191 Laurelview Court, Fremont, CA 94538
Thailand:     390/21 Moo 2, Khao Khan Song, Sriracha, Chonburi 20110
China(HQ):  No.510 Wulian Avenue, Chengdu 610200
E-mail:         sales@eoptolink.com 

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/eoptolink-launches-1-6t-osfp-2vr4-transceivers-using-200g-vcsels-302414876.html

SOURCE Eoptolink Technology Inc., Ltd.

data.title: Stocks are on pace for their worst month since 2022. Could April bring buying opportunities?
data.description:
By Isabel Wang

Seasonality and bearish sentiment could create an opportunity for investors next month - but policy uncertainty remains

The stock market's selloff in March may have felt like a never-ending nightmare.

U.S. stocks are on pace to wrap up a dismal March, as the volatility that has recently gripped Wall Street has shown no signs of abating. Tumbling over 8% as of Friday afternoon, the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite COMP is headed for its biggest monthly decline since December 2022. The S&P 500 SPX has slumped 6.3% in the same period - on track for its worst month since September 2022 - while the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA is off 5.2% on the month, according to FactSet data.

The sharp selloff has come as President Donald Trump has ratcheted up trade tensions with America's major trading partners. Earlier this month, Trump officially levied 25% tariffs against Mexico, Canada and China. Just two days later, the president confirmed the U.S. would pause tariffs on goods and services compliant with the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) until April 2. The Trump administration has referred to that day as "Liberation Day," when the president has promised to impose so-called retaliatory tariffs on all U.S. trading partners.

In the latest escalation of his trade war, Trump on Wednesday announced 25% tariffs on all foreign-made automobiles, which he said would take effect on April 3.

In turn, the Trump administration's frequent shifts on its tariff policies have sent financial markets into a tailspin, leaving investors uncertain about the direction of trade policy and, more importantly, its economic fallout. The rapid selloff also pushed the Nasdaq and the S&P 500 into correction territory earlier this month - defined as a 10% decline from a recent peak.

The U.S. stock market briefly breathed a sigh of relief last week after the Federal Reserve maintained its forecast for two interest-rate cuts this year. But that optimism proved short-lived as markets quickly retreated after data showed consumer sentiment had worsened amid resurgent inflation fears.

"The first half of the month was an emotional selloff in the stock market that, incredibly fast, moved from record high to correction. Now what we're seeing is confusion, and markets are going sideways," said Mark Hackett, chief market strategist at Nationwide Investment Management Group.

"This looks a lot like the pendulum swinging way too far in one direction, and setting the stage for what I expect to be a recovery," he told MarketWatch via phone on Friday.

April seasonality

To be sure, investors want to know how the sharp selloff in March sets the stage for April - one of the best-performing months of the year for U.S. stocks - and whether that strong seasonal trend could create more buying opportunities for investors.

History shows that, since 1971, April has been the second-best month of the year for the three major U.S. stock indexes, according to the Stock Trader's Almanac. In postelection years since 1950, April has remained a top-performing month - historically ranking as the second-best month for the Dow and the S&P 500, and the third-best month for the Nasdaq (see chart below).

Extreme bearish sentiment creates buying opportunity

Other indicators also suggest most of the pain could be behind us. Investor sentiment, as measured by the American Association for Individual Investors, has reached extremely bearish levels, presenting "credible buying opportunities" for investors, Hackett said.

Bearish sentiment - the expectation that stock prices will fall over the next six months - eased to 52.2% for the week ending March 26, according to the latest reading from the AAII, from 58.1% the week prior. This is the third time in the history of the survey that bearish sentiment has exceeded 50% for five consecutive weeks.

In Hackett's view, when sentiment has been this stretched, the S&P 500 has historically posted strong gains over the following six and 12 months.

The AAII's investor-sentiment survey is considered a contrarian indicator for the stock market - suggesting that it may be a good time to go in the opposite direction of the herd. Extreme bearishness often precedes upward movement in the stock market; conversely, extreme bullish sentiment could be a cue to sell.

Disconnect between sentiment and economic data

It's also worth noting that a clear disconnect still persists between consumer sentiment and economic data on consumer behavior, making it harder for investors to read the actual state of the economy.

Economic data on Friday showed consumer sentiment in March fell to the lowest level in over two years, according to the University of Michigan's consumer-sentiment survey. Meanwhile, personal spending still increased 0.4% in February, according to the personal-consumption expenditures (PCE) price index - but economists polled by the Wall Street Journal had forecast that spending would rise 0.5%.

See: Stocks fall sharply to end the week. 'Stagflation' fears are back in the spotlight.

Despite seasonal and contrarian tailwinds, what April holds for the stock market is still anyone's guess, especially with President Trump's April 2 tariff deadline looming. Investors hope that day could clear a fog of policy uncertainty that has clouded the stock market for months.

"Picking stock-market bottoms is very difficult. ... If you add the April 2 tariff deadline, and the fact that we're moving into earnings season, and the fact that a 'V-shaped' recovery for the stock market is fairly rare, those things tell me that we're probably not out of the woods yet in terms of volatility," Hackett said.

U.S. stocks tumbled on Friday, with the Dow falling over 700 points, or 1.7%, while the S&P 500 was off nearly 2% and the Nasdaq slumped 2.7%, according to FactSet data.

-Isabel Wang

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  03-29-25 0700ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: 'I'm absolutely terrible at managing money': I'm 35 with zero savings and $8,000 debt. How do I turn my life around?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I make about $2,800 a month and an extra $200 to $800 on freelancer work a month'

Dear Quentin,

I'm 35 years old. I have zero retirement savings, zero savings, about $8,000 worth of debt and now the phone company has dinged my credit seven times so that's gone down the drain as well.

I am absolutely terrible at managing money. I need help, I need someone to lead me in the right direction and to tell me how on earth to do and how to get on top of this. It's driving me crazy. I've been terrible with money all my life.

I make about $2,800 a month and an extra $200 to $800 on freelancer work a month (it varies a lot, I've gotten thousands some months and nothing in others). I want to get into a financial position where I've got decent retirement savings.

Thirtysomething

Related: 'I'll retire when I'm dead': My 401(k) lost $50,000 in the market turmoil. I'm in my early 40s. What should I do now?

Dear Thirtysomething,

You're not absolutely terrible at managing money. You've made decisions that didn't serve you. But you can learn from those decisions and become absolutely great at managing money.

Write a budget of your income and expenditures and be ruthless about it. Don't eat out. Don't get cabs. Take a break from your usual leisure activities and spend more time in your local library reading free books instead. Most people have not reached their peak earning power at 35. Fully fund your retirement account, if you have one, pay down student debt, and make sure you have an emergency fund of six to 12 months of expenses.

Fidelity Investments suggests that you should have double your salary saved for retirement by the time you're in your mid-30s and three times by the time you're in your mid-40s. These kinds of goals usually freak people out because millions of them are just trying to make it to their next payday. How many people actually lived up to those ideals? Not a lot. Don't believe the hype. Use it as a guide, but we are all on our own trajectory.

The truth is you are, to a large extent, competing with your own fears and anxieties. You can really only focus on one financial goal at a time, one step at a time.

Assuming you don't have a job with a 401(k), it's tough to strike a balance between planning for retirement and believing that you are so far behind that you will never catch up. An employer match adds fuel to your retirement-savings rocket. But the truth is you are, to a large extent, competing with your own fears and anxieties. You can really only focus on one financial goal at a time, one step at a time.

You will actually be saving money by paying off your $8,000 debt, as you are losing money with the amount of interest you're paying. Annual inflation is currently running at 2.8%, and you probably have more than 20% interest on your credit-card debt. You are, however, carrying a credit-card balance in line with the average American, although that figure varies by state. Americans overall are carrying $1.2 trillion in credit-card debt.

Related: Trump's trade war has rattled investors - uncertainty is a call to action

There are two main methods of paying off your debt: the snowball method (pay the lowest debt first) and the avalanche method (pay debt with the highest interest rate first). The former is usually a way to motivate people to get out of the red, but paying down the highest rate first makes the most sense to me. For example, if $7,000 of your $8,000 of your debt is credit-card debt, I'd be putting the entire amount to get rid of that debt.

You don't know what's around the corner. One job change or promotion could be enough to boost your income and set you on the road to financial wellness. The economy grows in cycles and you may - in 5 or 10 years from now - find yourself in a position to get a foot on the property ladder, if you haven't already. Your life will only get bigger and have new experiences. There is so much ahead for you, and you are preparing for that unknown.

You will restore balance to your life, but you need to ask these questions to ensure you don't make the same mistakes again. It's time to break that cycle.

You also need to ask yourself some hard questions, in addition to cutting down on your spending to address your debt. Why did you put so much money on plastic, assuming this is credit-card debt rather than student debt or medical debt? What has led you to this lack of equilibrium? You will restore balance to your life, but you need to ask these questions to ensure you don't make the same mistakes again. It's time to break that cycle.

It's not my intention to rake you over the coals. It's my hope that this conversation spurs you to ask these questions and find the answers for yourself so you can (a) understand how you found yourself at this financial crossroads and (b) feel empowered to change it, given that you do have the means to do so. Unless we acknowledge that our emotional lives and our financial lives are connected, we are doomed to repeat the same mistakes again.

Thus far, 2025 has been a rough ride for lots of people. At 35, you have 30 years before you retire. You have plenty of time to turn this ship around - or board a new ship.

Related: 'It's been a scary ride': My family has $800K in stocks. We lost 2 years of market gains in a few weeks. Do we sell - or buy?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'I'm deeply disturbed': My portfolio lost 20%. With Trump's trade war, do I sell my stocks and buy gold?

My husband has dementia and will need care. Will Medicaid go after my money if I use it to pay off our mortgage?

My stepmother inherited 100% of my father's estate. She's leaving everything to her two kids. Is that fair?

The Moneyist regrets he cannot respond to letters individually.Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  03-29-25 0630ET

data.title: I met a friend for lunch. When the check arrived, she said, 'Thank you so much for paying!' Was I taken for a fool?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'She had just completed four years of college and I had chosen the path of working in corporate America instead'

Dear Quentin,

I just read your reply about the carpooler who wondered whether they should contribute to the gas even though the driver would have his expenses reimbursed by the company. You also shared a story about a friend who took the check to have his employer reimburse it, even though you split the bill 50/50.

Since you like the tea, I thought I might share a story that happened to me. A female high-school friend and I had decided to reunite and have lunch. She was always very kind and I thought we could have a lasting friendship. I hadn't seen her in a couple years and thought this could be fun.

She had just completed four years of college and I had chosen the path of working in corporate America instead. She was employed at a local high school as a teacher and I was working at the local television station in sales for two years. We both chose the restaurant and had a great lunch. It was so nice to catch up on our families and everything.

Then the bill came. Since this was not a "date" it should have been assumed we would split the bill, right? I never carried cash (still don't) and pulled out my Amex card to pay my half. To my complete surprise, she stood up and declared (I will NEVER forget this), "Oh, thank you so much for paying! It was great to see you!" and out the door she went.

What in hell just happened? When was it assumed I was her cash register? I had no expense report, and the bill was all mine. Well, thanks. I just looked her up (literally yesterday) and she's been at that same high school.

I imagine her income is up there. I should call her, set up lunch and walk out - leaving her with the bill. Yes, like you, we never forget. I hate that - I wish I could forget. Was I taken for a fool?

The Former Friend

Related: 'He claims to be a nihilist': I told my friend to sell his Tesla shares. He did not appreciate my advice. Was I wrong?

Dear Friend,

I've heard of Irish exits, but this one takes the briosca. (That's Gaelic for biscuit.)

Friendships exist for many reasons. Usually, you have things in common - school, work, college, neighborhood or even politics (and, if you don't have politics in common, you generally refrain from arguing about it). You make each other laugh and/or you are sensitive to each other's feelings and have smart insights into each other's experiences. The litmus test is that you should, ideally, feel more - not less - energized and happy after you meet. That's usually because your personalities complement each other. It's a meeting of minds that's written in the stars.

But external influences can upset the apple cart, including social status, jobs and especially money. Here's a bizarre and slightly weird "statistic," courtesy of Bread Financial: (BFH) 21% of respondents in a survey say they have lost a friendship over money, while 26% said they feel they are financially incompatible with their friends. Money can create division, jealousy, feelings of insecurity and envy and, as you experienced, expectation and false assumption. You were working in corporate America, presumably making a good living; your friend had just started as a high-school teacher.

Our experiences have one thing in common: our respective silence. I had dinner with a friend who took the receipt for his work expenses after I paid 50% of the bill. I could have spoken up and said, "If you're getting reimbursed for this dinner, the least you can do is pay for the entire meal. Otherwise, you're being a cheapskate." I weighed up the price of the meal and the awkwardness of the interaction, and decided to let it slide. That may have been a mistake given that I still remember it all these years later. You, too, sat in shock and awe as your friend turned on her heels and left.

The biggest revelation I had reading your letter: It's time for us to let these experiences with penny-pinching friends go!

But these incidents also differ in one important way: intention. Your friend could have genuinely believed that you were picking up the tab. It may have been presumptuous, but it could have been a misunderstanding; her mistake was to jump to conclusions prematurely in good faith. My fellow definitely intended to "double dip" - allow me to pay for my bill even though he was taking the receipt to get reimbursed by his employer. But the biggest revelation I had reading your letter: It's time for us to let it go!

Your experience also raises an uncomfortable financial issue for both of you. She had just started as a high-school teacher and, after four years of college, was likely not in as good financial shape as you. The average annual wage for high-school teachers hovers around $73,800. The annual salary ranges from $48,040 to $106,380, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics. In other words, your friend could have believed you were taking her out to lunch, given her (compared to you) modest postcollege wage.

I've had quite a few letters over the years about splitting the check. There was the man whose friend sucker-punched him into paying for a $150 sushi feast because his friend had wrangled two free tickets to a concert, and he expected to be treated royally for the favor. The reason that worked is because we are often at our most socially anxious when we're sitting in a restaurant, which is one reason - and this is a theory - people act strangely, speak loudly and appear to perform for the other guests. (Another reason is the consumption of alcohol.)

Your friend sounds like a good egg. I like that she has been at the same high school for many years. Teaching kids is a fulfilling yet tough job, and not everyone is cut out for it. Teachers earn every one of their days off in the summer (for those who can afford to take summers off, that is, and not work side hustles to earn extra dough). For all our analysis and reflecting on past matters of financial etiquette, I have a feeling that if we met our respective friends again, neither of them would even remember.

Let's both raise an imaginary glass of champagne to them, put it on our virtual tabs, and wish them well.

Related: Should I wear my Trump MAGA hat to work?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'I can't deal with managing them': I juggle 18 credit cards. How do I close them without ruining my credit score?

'It's the saddest thing': I'm happily retired and my friends in their 60s want to know how I did it. Should I tell them my secret?

'My father was a teetotaling, fair-minded, liberal-leaning capitalist': And yet I allowed 'friends' to borrow $120K. How do I deal with the fallout?

-Quentin Fottrell

data.title: KIIT Expands Global Presence Through Strategic Academic Collaborations
data.description:
KIIT Expands Global Presence Through Strategic Academic Collaborations

KIIT Expands Global Presence Through Strategic Academic Collaborations

PR Newswire

BHUBANESWAR, India, March 29, 2025

BHUBANESWAR, India, March 29, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- KIIT Deemed to be University (https://kiit.ac.in/), Bhubaneswar, India, continues to expand its global footprint through strategic partnerships with reputed international institutions. It has recently signed Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) with three prestigious universities - the University of Tulsa (USA), Toi Ohomai Institute of Technology (New Zealand), and the University of Washington-Bothell (USA). These collaborations offer students and faculty enhanced academic and research opportunities.

As part of its global education initiative, KIIT has partnered with the University of Tulsa, Oklahoma, USA, to launch a dual degree program in B.Tech. This 2+2 model allows students to complete two years at KIIT and the remaining two years at the University of Tulsa, earning a degree from both institutions.

Beyond the dual degree program, 40 KIIT students from various disciplines, including engineering and liberal studies, will participate in short-term courses at the University of Tulsa, gaining hands-on experience and international exposure. This partnership also facilitates faculty exchanges, joint research projects, and sabbatical opportunities between the two institutions.

Further strengthening its international presence, KIIT has signed an MoU with Toi Ohomai Institute of Technology, New Zealand. Signed in the presence of Mr. Christopher Luxon, Hon'ble Prime Minister of New Zealand, the partnership aims to enhance teaching and research capabilities, focusing on articulation pathways in commerce and management at both undergraduate and postgraduate levels. Toi Ohomai is a leading vocational and applied tertiary education provider known for its industry-aligned programs and hands-on learning approach.

KIIT has also forged an impactful academic collaboration with the University of Washington-Bothell (UWB), USA, through an immersive learning initiative. A delegation of 18 members from UWB, during their visit to KIIT, worked with KIIT's management students to analyze real-world business challenges faced by local enterprises and develop sustainable solutions.

This initiative brought together students from both universities, providing them with valuable international exposure and practical learning experiences. The collaboration represents an innovative model for global academic engagement, encouraging universities to move beyond traditional exchange programs and embrace collaborative teaching models.

With over 350 MoUs signed with leading universities and corporations worldwide, KIIT has established itself as a hub for academic excellence and cultural exchange. Dr. Achyuta Samanta, Founder, KIIT, KISS & KIMS, emphasized the importance of such collaborations in equipping students with a global perspective, AI integration, and skill development opportunities that align with the evolving global workforce.

Logo -  https://mma.prnewswire.com/media/2234144/5241906/KIIT_Logo.jpg

data.title: 'She has been telling him lies': My sister convinced my father to sign everything over to her. What can I do?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'She has brought chaos and stress into everyone's life'

Dear Quentin,

I'm not sure where to begin. I am one of three sisters. My youngest sister and I found out that our eldest sister has been manipulating our father, and she has been telling him lies about my other sister and me for a very long time. My other sister and I left home, and did as best as we could financially.

Our eldest sister was a financial drain on our father, and he bailed her out. But he also coddled, enabled and defended her mean and neurotic behavior. Our father has dementia and it's worse now than we thought. He hasn't been formally diagnosed as he doesn't trust doctors.

We found out that our eldest sister made our father sign a document. He told us numerous times that he didn't know what it was. He hadn't spoken to the lawyer that drew up these documents, nor did he even request them.

The eldest sister just made our father sign these documents that leave her everything that he owns, and I mean everything: two properties, a car and bank accounts. It's a joint tenancy with just the two of them on the document.

Dubious legal documents

This sister never told my other sister and me anything. We found out because our father went to my other sister (months after the documents were signed) very upset because he realized he had signed these documents and didn't know what they were.

Obviously, our father wants there to be "no hard feelings" between us. We are two responsible siblings and have a good relationship with most everyone in the family, including our mom when she was still alive - while the other toxic sister had MANY conflicts with our mom.

Our eldest sister has so much control over him that now he's saying that this is what he wants, and that the eldest sister was unfairly treated when our mother died and wasn't left anything, so he wants to help her more. She seems to forget that she treated our mom horribly.

When the time comes, my other sister and I would still be fine with liquidating the assets and dividing them equally three ways between the three sisters. But our oldest sister just wants everything. She has brought chaos and stress into everyone's life.

Disappointed & Discouraged

Related: 'In their last days, our parents changed their will': They left me $250,000, but gave my sister $1 million. What should I do?

Dear Disappointed,

There's an old saying: Possession is nine-tenths of the law - well, so is preparation.

By your telling, your father has been bullied, ambushed and coerced into signing a new will. There are two people at fault here: Your eldest sister and the attorney she used to draft these documents without the express cooperation and/or permission of your father.

As sometimes happens when there is a troubled adult child, the relationship can start out as a codependent one and, as that parent ages and becomes more vulnerable, it can progress into financial elder abuse. Leaving everything to your eldest sister would be a red flag for any judge.

An elder-care attorney can advise you on the best legal strategy, but I suggest you strike now while your father is alive, and not wait until he dies for your sister to clean out his bank accounts and inherit his home. It will involve him being evaluated by a doctor for his cognitive health.

Your ultimate goal is to have a court rule that any legal documents he signed are not valid and/or have yourself or your other sister appointed with a durable power of attorney. You may also need to instigate proceedings for your father to be put in a conservatorship.

This is especially important if he needs long-term care, which will be expensive. He may, for now, resist seeing a doctor, but there may come a point if and/or when he is declared legally incapacitated that he will have to be evaluated.

A medical evaluation

"A doctor cannot go against a person's wishes unless a court declares the person legally incapacitated or the person's wishes are medically or ethically inappropriate," according to the consumer edition of the Merck Manual, the medical reference book.

And if you suspect your father lacks testamentary capacity? "If the person or other appropriate party objects to a particular medical decision or to the determination of clinical incapacity, the courts may become involved," the manual says.

It's easier to do so if there's an emergency. "People are presumed to consent to any necessary emergency treatment," it adds. "This process of making emergency healthcare decisions for people who cannot make decisions for themselves is rarely litigated in court."

As for the will and other legal documents signed by your father: He may be scared of your eldest sister, which is why he reneged on his earlier statement and said that he knew what he was doing, even though you are 99% sure he had no clue.

Taking legal action

This will be easier to prove, in theory, while your father is alive. "A testator must have the mental capacity to understand the nature and consequences of their actions when creating a will," according to the Law Office of Stephen Bilkis & Associates, which has offices in New York.

"This means that they must have the ability to understand the property they own, the persons who would normally inherit their property, and the effect of the will they are making," it adds. "Testamentary capacity is important to ensure that a will is considered valid."

Finally, you can and probably should report the attorney your sister reportedly enlisted to draw up these documents to the state bar association, and send a letter to the financial institution that is managing his funds reminding them of their responsibility.

You have a hard decision to make about taking your sister to task. The National Center on Elder Abuse, a government agency affiliated with the U.S. Administration on Aging, and the nonprofit National Adult Protective Services Association will have further steps you can take.

You can also visit the Eldercare Locator website or call 1-800-677-1116 to report concerns. Elder abuse, including neglect and exploitation, is experienced by about one in 10 older adults age 60 and over who live at home, according to the CDC.

It's time to meet chaos with the sobering arm of the law.

Related: 'He claims to be a nihilist': I told my friend to sell his Tesla shares. He stopped speaking to me. Is that normal?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, tell me what you want to know more about, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

'I'm not sure what to do': My husband has dementia and needs care. Will Medicaid go after our money if I use it to pay off our mortgage?

My stepmother inherited 100% of my father's estate. She's leaving everything to her two kids. Is that fair?

I invested $4,000 in 2020 when the market crashed. It's now worth $55,000 with 65% in Nvidia. Am I now in trouble?

-Quentin Fottrell

data.title: Everything you wanted to know about a recession but were afraid to ask
data.description:
By Greg Robb

Economists are slashing their forecasts for growth and beginning to raise their odds of an economic downturn

Over the last two months, companies and consumers have signaled so much concern about the outlook for their businesses and pocketbooks that economists are slashing their forecasts for growth and beginning to raise their odds of a recession.

With President Donald Trump stoking economic uncertainty by implementing wide-ranging tariffs, a steep slowdown in growth in the next several months now seems inevitable. Whether there is a recession - a period of time when the economy basically falls off a cliff - remains a matter of debate.

Surveys that ask people questions and analyze their answers are referred to as "soft" data - and so far they haven't translated into "hard data," cold numbers like job growth, retail sales and consumer spending. The hard data is holding up well two month into President Donald Trump's second-term.

Soft data doesn't automatically mean a recession, but it is a necessary condition for the hard data to go sideways, said Mark Zandi, chief economist at Moody's Analytics, in an interview.

"You don't go from everyone's feeling great to a recession," Zandi said, in an interview. You go from where everyone's feeling great, to where they are not so good, to recession," he said.

Wall Street economists are loath to forecast a recession. Gregory Daco, chief economist at EY Parthenon, sees the odds of a recession at 40%. In normal times, the odds of a recession are seen as around 15%.

Prominent independent economists are more worried in public about the outlook. "If I had to make a bet, it would be a recession," said former Fed Vice Chairman Alan Blinder in a CNBC interview Wednesday . "Not a titanic one, but negative GDP growth is more likely than not."

Since 2022, there have been periods of false alarms about recessions as many economists thought a recession was inevitable as the U.S. central bank had to quickly raise interest rates to cool inflation. But the predicted recession never happened. This has led to this bit of a sense of the-boy-who-cried-wolf about a recession.

One good way to think of a recession is as a downward spiral of growth that can take on a life of its own. Typically caused by a shock or financial stress, consumes and businesses pull back. In the face of fewer sales, companies start to let go of workers, which can cause even more economic weakness. For economists, a recession represents a sharp break in their models that focus on trends. This is what makes them so hard to predict. It is hard to economists to say when the models they use will suddenly not work.

Read: Is a recession brewing? These first-time homeowners will signal it first, one economist says.

It is difficult to understate the damage of recessions. For no fault of their own, workers suddenly lose their jobs and income. The government ramps up spending by issuing more debt in order to try to stabilize the economy but the U.S. social safety net is frayed.

The general rule-of-thumb is that two consecutive quarters of GDP growth is a recession. But that's not always the case. There is a nonprofit National Bureau of Economic Research, that officially decides whether the U.S. has entered a recession by looking at various measures of economic activity.

Trump tariffs

Could a recession actually happen this time? Here's everything we know.

Right now, the most remarkable feature of the economy, experts said, is that uncertainty has descended like a fog over the economy. It is hard to pick up a signal of a trend from the noise. "I just think uncertainty is remarkably high," is how Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell summed up the mood at his press conference last week.

That means the fleet of Ph.D. economists at the U.S. central bank are having trouble seeing the path forward. To start with, it is not clear what President Trump's tariff picture will look like in six months. Republicans are also playing chicken with the debt ceiling, creating uncertainty on Wall Street.

At the same time, investors are moving to buy gold and bitcoin - taking scarce private savings away from productive investments. And the Fed has signaled it won't ease monetary policy to protect the economy and is on hold until the fog clears.

"All of this amounts to an economy that has resilience but is listing over in the first quarter," said Brian Bethune, an economist at Boston College. "The more it lists under uncertainty, the more likely a rogue wave comes along, capsizing the ship into recession," he added.

The shift in confidence in the last couple months is extraordinary.

"I've been at the Fed for 10 years and this is the most dramatic shift in confidence that I can recall except for when COVID hit in March 2020," said Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari, in a speech in rural Michigan on Wednesday.

This diminishing confidence among both corporate bosses and American shoppers has raised the risk that businesses and consumers pull back at the same time,.this would dramatically weaken the economy because everyone stops spending and investing in new factories, he said.

The good news, Kashkari said, is that confidence could be restored quickly if resolution to trade uncertainties is resolved. But "the longer this weak confidence lasts, the more meaningful it becomes," Kashkari warned.

Economists already see some warning signs on the consumer front. Mike Feroli, chief economist at JP Morgan Chase, (JPM) pointed out in a recent research note that restaurant sales have just had their worst three months in three years, including a large 1.5% drop in February.

"Given the discretionary nature of eating out, it's plausible that this is one early sign of households tightening their belts, Feroli wrote.

DOGE and Elon Musk

The concerns about the outlook are not only about tariffs. The March consumer confidence data showed a spike in job uncertainty. And consumer optimism about future income largely vanished.

What's behind the gloomy expectations?

Economists said it was possible that the sweeping cuts that Elon Musk's DOGE is making to the federal workforce is putting a chill in private-sector employees across the country, giving them another reason to save and not spend.

"It may be reminding people of the tenuous nature of their own jobs," said Zandi.

"Workers are bound to ask themselves, 'If this is what Elon Musk, the best business person on the planet, does and this is deemed to be good business practice, what does it mean for my job'?" Zandi said.

Consumer spending makes up 70% of gross domestic product. Ajay Rajadhyaksha, global chairman of research at Barclays, said that the low savings rate for consumers is the "Achilles heel" for the U.S. economy.

The savings rate has been "extraordinarily low," in the range of around 4% since COVID, down from 7% pre-pandemic, as consumers have used excess savings to spent money on experiences.

"Now, there is a constant drumbeat of DOGE job cuts, policy uncertainty, actually rising tariffs, and equity markets pulling back. It is not crazy to assume the savings rate goes up," he said.

If the savings rate went up a full percentage point or more, that would be 100 basis points subtracted from consumption and GDP growth, he noted.

Rajadhyaksha said a recession wasn't his baseline call. But he said there would be a dramatic slowdown this year to a 0.7% growth rate on a fourth-quarter over fourth-quarter basis, down from 2.5% rate last year.

When economists talk about growth below 1%, all it takes is a stiff breeze to push the economy into a ditch.

'Peak pessimism'

Thomas Simons, chief U.S. economist at Jefferies, is one of the economists who think recession fears are overblown. "I broadly don't buy the whole recession story," Simons said, in an interview.

Despite trouble among low-income workers, overall household balance sheets are in an extremely strong position, he said.

"There is nowhere near the vulnerability that would cause a spiraling situation of deflation of assets and money supply and wealth destruction that we saw in 2008," he said.

While he agrees there is a risk that the economy will be soft until June, he doesn't think that conditions are ripe for a destructive full-blown recession.

Simons said we're now at "peak pessimism" about Trump's economic policies, and it will wane over coming months.

"The economy overall, in my view, is on a pace with enough momentum to withstand a little bit of a pullback in spending," he said.

Economic growth in the first quarter will be quite weak, and only modestly better in the April -June quarter. But the economy will snap back to better growth in the second half of the year.

Read next: Thinking about selling your stocks? Here's something to keep in mind.

Jim Glassman, a former economist at JPMorgan Chase, agrees with Simons that the economy is structurally sound. He noted that the government reported Thursday that global earnings for U.S. headquartered operations jumped 25.6% in the fourth quarter of last year and 6.8% over the entire year.

"Beyond the frenzy over uncertainty, there are solid business fundamentals," Glassman said, in an email.

-Greg Robb

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  03-29-25 0529ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: My father died, leaving everything to my 90-year-old stepmother. Do I have a right to ask her if I'm in her will?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I had a good relationship with her as a child but do not see her much as an adult'

Dear Quentin,

My stepmother appointed my stepbrother as the executor of her will. My father passed away in 2004. My stepmother has a condo and a fair amount of investments, although I have no idea how much she has. She was always good with money, and she squeezed the value out of every dollar bill until George Washington's eyes popped out.

I had a good relationship with her as a child but do not see her much as an adult. I'm in my 60s, and she's 90 or thereabouts. My stepbrother is also my business partner. We see each other rarely nowadays (he has another business he runs) but we chat weekly. Although we don't talk about it much, he has indicated there would be nothing for me in her will.

'She squeezed the value out of every dollar bill until George Washington's eyes popped out.'

I guess I feel a bit slighted, because the two biological children that she had with my dad will inherit everything. Realistically, I don't need the money, but he was my dad too. He was the wage earner in our family. I have considered asking her directly, but that will be an awkward conversation for sure, and I'm not sure I want to go there.

It's the not knowing for sure that bothers me the most. Most likely, I don't have a legal leg to stand on to contest anything, but what has been your experience with situations like this? I don't want to drive a wedge between me and my brother, but I'd love to know the specifics before she passes away. Just curious as to your thoughts.

Slighted Stepson

Related: 'I feel like I've opened a hornet's nest': My mother-in-law lists my husband as beneficiary on her $1 million IRA. But her will says otherwise. Who wins?

Dear Slighted,

I have good news and bad news. The good news is that you don't need the inheritance to pay for food, a place to live, an education or a new car. The bad news is that you don't have a current relationship with your stepmother, despite her having been like a mother to you when you were growing up and the fact that your two half-siblings are also her biological children.

For the most part, we maintain relationships with people who mean a great deal to us. Your stepmother will have gotten that memo from you a long time ago, and perhaps your half-brother's comments were based on the fact that you and your stepmother have not maintained a relationship. If you don't call on her birthday or take trips to see her, she may not care to remember you in her will.

In other words, you didn't invest in the relationship, but you still want to reap the rewards. It doesn't mean you're a bad person or she's an uncaring person, but even children who have two biological parents can't expect to receive an inheritance simply because they believe it's their right. Parents can cut their kids out of their will anytime. And they do.

You didn't invest in the relationship, but you still want to reap the rewards.

As to whether you should ask her about her will: Put yourself in her place and imagine your own reaction if you had a stepson who, after years of not being in touch, arrived on your doorstep or called you to ask about your will. You're not pretending that you're getting in touch because you suddenly realized you missed her. My guess is that in her place, you would think, "Now he wants to talk to me!"

Approach your stepmother only under these two conditions: first, if you have something else to bring to the table, and second, if you genuinely don't expect an inheritance and can let go of the possibility of receiving one. Otherwise, you are embarking on a negotiation and, assuming there are no trusts that list you as a beneficiary, your stepmother holds all the cards.

You don't have to keep a ledger of the mistakes she might have made in your relationship. You only need to account for your own. So you could be honest with her and use the conversation with your half-brother as a starting point: "Justin told me that I should not expect anything in your will. I don't need money at this point in my life, but it made me sad because we had lost touch."

If you were 30 and she were 60, you might have more reason to expect some money from your father's estate. You would be at a point where you were starting out in life, perhaps saving for a down payment for a house, and there would still be time to save or build some kind of relationship beyond a mere transactional one. But now you are in your 60s and she's in her 90s.

The last thing she needs right now is a confrontation.

Related: 'My wife and I are very grateful': Our son wants to pay off our mortgage before we retire. Will this backfire?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com.

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, tell me what you want to know more about, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

'She acted as a mother to me growing up': My stepmother remarried after my father died. How can I claim my inheritance?

'I believe myself to be an honorable person': Do I have the right to ask my husband if I'll inherit his house after he dies?

'Is this ethical?' I want to leave my home to my children from my first marriage - and not to my second husband.

-Quentin Fottrell
